opportunities.event_name: US Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index — February 2026
opportunities.rationale: PCE is the Fed's preferred inflation gauge, released 10 days after FOMC held at 3.5-3.75% and raised PCE forecast to 2.7%. With WTI at $98 driving energy cost pass-through, a hot print (core PCE >0.4% MoM) would kill remaining rate-cut hopes and send US500 toward 6,300; a soft print (<0.2%) would trigger a relief rally toward 6,650 as 1-cut pricing firms. S&P 500 at 6,506 sits below 50-day MA (6,818) — key technical reference. Invalidated if VIX drops below 20 before release, suggesting regime shift.

opportunities.event_name: US Non-Farm Payrolls & Unemployment Rate — March 2026
opportunities.rationale: February NFP printed a soft 92K, sharply below expectations. March data will confirm whether the labour market is genuinely weakening under oil-driven stagflation or if Feb was a weather/seasonal anomaly. A sub-100K print would accelerate recession pricing and send US500 toward 6,200-6,300; a strong rebound (>200K) eases growth fears but keeps Fed on hold. S&P at 6,506, below 50-day MA 6,818 — bear trend intact. Invalidated if Iran ceasefire materially shifts risk appetite before release.

opportunities.event_name: OPEC+ Joint Ministerial Monitoring Committee Meeting
opportunities.trade_setup: volatility spike — direction dependent on output decision
opportunities.rationale: With Strait of Hormuz disrupted and WTI at $98, OPEC+ faces a binary decision: increase output to offset Iran supply loss (bearish oil, bullish equities) or maintain cuts to weaponize scarcity (bullish oil toward $110+, bearish equities). Goldman Sachs Brent target $110. Saudi Arabia's response is the key variable. USOIL at $98 with 52-week high near $100 — a supply boost would break the rally, output hold sends it to $105-110. Invalidated if Hormuz reopens before meeting.

Eurozone inflation is under dual pressure: oil-driven energy costs (WTI $98) pushing headline CPI higher vs weak domestic demand pulling core lower. A hot print (>2.8% YoY) constrains ECB easing and supports EUR/USD above 1.16; a soft print (<2.3%) opens door for April cut and pressures EUR toward 1.14. EUR/USD at 1.159, hovering near 50-day support. Invalidated if US PCE on Mar 28 causes such a large USD move that EU CPI reaction is muted.

ISM Manufacturing prints the same day as OPEC+ meeting, creating a double-catalyst session. A sub-50 contraction reading would confirm the stagflation narrative (rising input prices from oil + shrinking activity) and accelerate equity selling. Expansion above 50 with tame prices-paid sub-component would be the most bullish combo. S&P at 6,506 below 50-day MA (6,818). Invalidated if OPEC+ headline dominates and ISM reaction is negligible.

opportunities.trade_setup: pullback buy on supply shock continuation
opportunities.entry_trigger: Enter long on dip into 94-97 zone on any temporary de-escalation noise or pre-OPEC+ profit-taking. Exit immediately on confirmed ceasefire + Hormuz reopening or OPEC+ emergency output increase >1.5Mbpd.
opportunities.rationale: Strait of Hormuz 70% traffic reduction is choking ~15Mbpd of global oil transit. Goldman Sachs target $110 Brent, JPMorgan warns sustained >$90 triggers 10-15% S&P correction. WTI at $98 near 52-week highs; a pullback into 94-97 (prior breakout zone above the $90 resistance shelf) offers R:R of 1:2.2 to $108. Stop at $89.50 is below the structural $90 level that held as resistance through Q4 2025. Invalidated by confirmed ceasefire or coordinated IEA SPR release.

opportunities.entry_trigger: Enter long only if gold holds above $4,350 for 2 consecutive daily closes AND RSI(14) daily turns up from sub-30 territory. Do NOT enter pre-PCE (Mar 28) — wait for post-data confirmation. Scale: 50% initial, add remaining 50% on reclaim of $4,600.
opportunities.rationale: Gold crashed 17% from $5,400 to $4,491 on USD strength + rate-cut repricing after FOMC signaled only 1 cut in 2026. However, the Iran war safe-haven bid and stagflation backdrop remain intact. The $4,350 area aligns with the November 2025 breakout level and the 200-day MA zone. R:R is 1:2.0 from midpoint ($4,425) to $4,850 (prior support-turned-resistance from early March). Stop below $4,180 respects the measured move target of the crash. Invalidated below $4,180 or if Fed signals 0 cuts.

opportunities.trade_setup: safe-haven USD continuation into event cluster
opportunities.entry_trigger: Enter short at current levels (1.158-1.165) with tight size ahead of PCE (Mar 28) and EU CPI (Mar 31). Add on hot US PCE or soft EU CPI. Exit on dovish Fed repricing or confirmed Iran de-escalation.
opportunities.rationale: EUR/USD at 1.159 is caught between safe-haven USD demand (Iran war, VIX 26.78) and Eurozone energy vulnerability (Germany oil-import dependent, WTI $98). Fed held at 3.5-3.75% with hawkish 1-cut dot plot; ECB under pressure to cut on weak growth. Rate differential favours USD. The 1.1650 level is prior structure resistance and 20-day EMA area. Target 1.1300 aligns with the January 2026 low. R:R is 1:1.9 from midpoint. Invalidated above 1.1750 (would signal EUR strength breakout).

opportunities.entry_trigger: Enter short on any bounce into 6,550-6,650 zone (prior support, now resistance). Requires daily close to remain below 50-day MA (6,818). Skip entry if VIX drops below 22 or Iran ceasefire is announced.
opportunities.rationale: S&P 500 at 6,506 is trading below the 50-day MA (6,818) in a confirmed downtrend with VIX at 26.78 and CNN Fear & Greed at 15 (Extreme Fear). The 6,550-6,650 zone was prior support that broke down and should act as resistance on any relief bounce. JPMorgan warns oil above $90 could trigger 10-15% correction from highs, implying 6,100-6,200 target. R:R of 1:2.5 from 6,600 midpoint. Conviction LOW because Extreme Fear readings are contrarian and a sharp relief rally is possible on any de-escalation headline. Stop above 6,750 (halfway to 50-day MA).
opportunities.regime_override_justification: Short direction aligns with high-vol downtrend regime. LOW conviction due to Extreme Fear contrarian risk.
